behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum
behaviors.justification: This tests a single built-in function's logic in isolation. The real failure (wrong sum) is caught by evaluating the formula once with a set of known values. No dependencies on other modules, filesystem, or real infrastructure. A pure function evaluation with test data.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 cells with known numeric values (1-10). Construct a formula object representing SUM(A1:A10). Call the formula evaluator and assert the result equals 55. Edge cases: empty range, single cell, negative numbers, floating-point values.

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell that contains a formula produces the correct cascaded result
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in dependency resolution: the engine must parse B1's formula (=A1*2), evaluate it, then use that result in C1's formula (=B1+1). A Unit test that mocks B1's value misses the real cascading logic. We must test the full engine-with-workbook interaction, where the engine resolves the reference graph and re-evaluates in dependency order.
behaviors.test_description: Create a 3-cell workbook: A1=5, B1==A1*2, C1==B1+1. Call the evaluator on C1. Assert result is 11. Verify that changing A1 to 10 causes C1 to recalculate to 21 (tests that the reference is live). Edge cases: longer chains (A→B→C→D), circular references during construction.

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency and returns a circular reference error
behaviors.justification: Circular detection requires building a real dependency graph and detecting cycles in it. Mocking the graph traversal would hide whether cycle detection actually works. The real failure is a hang or infinite loop when evaluating A1 that references B1 that references A1. We must construct the actual graph and test the detection logic end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Create two cells: A1==B1, B1==A1. Attempt to evaluate A1. Assert that a CircularReferenceError is returned, not an infinite loop or incorrect value. Edge cases: longer cycles (A→B→C→A), self-reference (A1==A1), partial cycles (A1→B1→A1→C1).

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with exact match returns correct value; returns error when key not found
behaviors.justification: VLOOKUP is a self-contained function that takes a lookup key, a table, and column index, and returns a value or error. The real failure (wrong value, missing error handling) is caught by testing the function logic in isolation with controlled input tables. No engine interdependencies or external resources.
behaviors.test_description: Test VLOOKUP function directly with a 2D array as lookup table. Case 1: key exists in first column, return value from specified column. Case 2: key does not exist, return #N/A error. Case 3: negative/out-of-bounds column index, return #REF! error. Edge cases: duplicate keys (return first match), case sensitivity, numeric vs string keys.

behaviors.description: IF function correctly handles nested conditions
behaviors.justification: IF is a logical function that evaluates a condition and returns one of two branches. Nested IF is just recursion within the function. The real failure (wrong branch chosen) is caught by testing the evaluator with controlled condition values. No dependencies on other modules or resources.
behaviors.test_description: Test IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low") with A1 values: 5 (→"low"), 15 (→"medium"), 25 (→"high"). Assert correct branch is chosen. Edge cases: nested IFs with boolean shortcuts, error values in conditions, mixed type comparisons.

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with separator, skips empty cells
behaviors.justification: CONCATENATE is a function that takes a list of values and joins them. The real failure (incorrect separator handling, empty cells producing blanks) is caught by testing the function with various input combinations. Pure logic, no interdependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Test CONCATENATE function with separator. Case 1: ["a", "b", "c"] with "-" returns "a-b-c". Case 2: ["a", "", "c"] with "-" returns "a-c" (empty skipped). Case 3: single value, multiple empty cells. Assert that empty cells do not create extra separators. Edge cases: null values, mixed types, very long strings.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet resolves correctly
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require the formula parser to recognize the Sheet!Cell syntax and the engine to resolve the reference across sheet boundaries. Mocking sheet lookup misses whether the actual parser and resolution logic work together. The real failure is the reference not being found or resolved incorrectly. We must test with a real multi-sheet workbook.
behaviors.test_description: Create two sheets: Sheet1 and Sheet2. Sheet2!A1 = 10. Sheet1 has B1 = =Sheet2!A1+5. Evaluate B1, assert result is 15. Test that changing Sheet2!A1 to 20 updates B1 to 25. Edge cases: missing sheet (return #REF!), invalid cell reference (Sheet2!Z999), circular cross-sheet references.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell changes, all dependent formulas recalculate in topological order
behaviors.justification: Recalculation order is critical: if C1 depends on B1 and B1 depends on A1, changing A1 must update B1 before C1, not vice versa. A unit test that mocks dependency tracking misses whether topological ordering is actually correct. Real failure: recalculation happens in wrong order, producing stale or inconsistent values. We must test the full dependency graph and recalculation engine.
behaviors.test_description: Create a chain: A1=5, B1==A1+10, C1==B1*2, D1==C1-B1. Set up listeners to track recalculation order. Change A1 to 6. Assert recalculation happens in order B1→C1→D1. Verify final values are correct (B1=16, C1=32, D1=16). Edge cases: diamond dependencies (A→B, A→C, B→D, C→D), wide fan-out (one cell affecting many).

behaviors.description: Undo operation restores both formula text and dependent values
behaviors.justification: Undo tests multiple subsystems together: the edit operation changes a formula, the undo stack captures state, recalculation updates dependents, and undo restores all of it. A unit test of just the undo stack misses whether dependent values are correctly restored. Real failure: after undo, formula is restored but dependents are stale. We need the full undo/edit/recalc engine.
behaviors.test_description: Create workbook with A1=5, B1==A1*2 (evaluates to 10). Edit B1 formula to ==A1*3. Assert B1 evaluates to 15. Call undo. Assert B1 formula reverts to ==A1*2 and value reverts to 10. Edge cases: undo after multiple edits, undo with circular reference introduced and removed, undo with sheet deletion.

behaviors.description: Saving to disk and loading back produces identical cell values and formulas, no data loss
behaviors.justification: Serialization correctness depends on actual filesystem I/O. Mocking file writes hides real failures: binary format corruption, floating-point precision loss, Unicode handling, or incomplete writes. Real failure: save a workbook, load it, values/formulas differ. Only real filesystem I/O catches this. Critical for data integrity.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with diverse data: integers, floats (3.14159), text, formulas (=A1+B1), cross-sheet refs (=Sheet2!A1), error values (#DIV/0!). Save to temporary file using the engine's binary format. Load from file into a new workbook object. Assert all cell values match exactly (including floating-point precision), all formula texts match, all sheet structure matches. Cleanup temp file. Edge cases: very large workbooks, special characters in strings, empty sheets, workbooks with no formulas.
